news.title: ‘A surprising success’: US, Mexico and Canada fans on hosting the World Cup
news.lede: The joy, love, hope and fun provided by fans outweighed the commercialism, extortionate prices and ugly politics. This summer has been so vibrant and full of life. I had my doubts but, as the World Cup approached and Boston got ready to host its first game, I started feeling the excitement and enthusiasm in the air. That mainly had to do with the huge numbers of Scottish fans who arrived for their match against Haiti.

news.title: Argentina embarrassed itself after losing to Spain in World Cup final
news.lede: There was almost never a doubt that Argentina was going to be upset and maybe even angry after losing to Spain 1-0 in the World Cup final. Sadly, the biggest question was really on how bad things would get toward the end of the match if Argentina was losing, but — as it turns out — it was more about how irate and hostile Argentina would be after the game that really stole show. While not as terrible as some of those clutching their pearls have made it out to be, it was a bit embarrassing, with a dose of being classless mixed in for the 2022 World Cup champions. Here is Fox’s look at part of what happened: [Media: https://twitter.com/FOXSports/status/2078969205018534039?

news.title: How World Cup 2026 proved football has remarkable ability to cure all ills
news.lede: The trophy safely on display at the Spanish FA headquarters, England’s wounds no longer so raw, and the annual football calendar now into its miniscule vacuum, you’re left to reflect on the past six weeks and ponder whether the 2026 edition in the United States, Canada and Mexico was a resounding success or an unmitigated flop. This one really could have gone either way. The expansion from 32 teams to 48 had the potential for greater drama but also the risk of suffocating, of making the World Cup the one thing it should never be: tedious. Would North America buy into soccer in a way we hadn’t seen at USA 1994?

news.title: 'I'm sorry': Why Argentina's assistant coach Roberto Ayala apologised after FIFA World Cup final
news.lede: NEW DELHI: Argentina assistant coach Roberto Ayala has apologised for his altercation with Spain midfielder Dani Olmo after the FIFA World Cup 2026 final, admitting that his actions were unacceptable. The ugly scenes unfolded after Spain defeated Argentina 1-0 in extra time on Sunday to lift the World Cup title. FIFA has since launched an investigation into the post-match brawl. Chaos after the final whistleTensions boiled over immediately after the final whistle as players and staff from both teams became involved in heated confrontations.Spain captain Rodri was seen arguing with Argentina defender Nicolas Otamendi.

news.title: Brazil's Repeat World Cup Wins: A Rare Football Feat
news.lede: Winning back-to-back FIFA World Cups is an extraordinary achievement that only two nations have managed, with decades passing since these occurrences. However, winning two out of three World Cups is also notable, with Brazil achieving this twice (1962 and 1970, then 1994 and 2002) and Argentina once (1978 and 1986). The significant eight-year gaps between these wins often resulted in vastly different team rosters. For instance, Brazil's 1962 team and its 1970 squad shared only the legendary Pele, who missed the 1962 final due to injury.

news.title: Arsenal defender Saliba sidelined for ‘extended period’ due to back injury
news.lede: Centre back William Saliba won't require surgery for injury sustained at World Cup, his club says.
